claim: Active livestock herding (goats, sheep, cows) occurs across multiple Algarve municipalities including Alcoutim, Tavira, Loulé, Silves, Monchique, and Vila do Bispo — herder presence creates human activity zones in otherwise remote areas that could yield lost dog sightings.
stance_target: herder/farmer activity as distributed human-presence network in rural Algarve
quoted_support: We meet goatherds and shepherds with their varied flocks in Alcoutim, Tavira, Loulé and in Silves, Monchique and Vila do Bispo... In between, you can cross paths with cows grazing freely in the hills of Monchique.

claim: Wild boar, foxes, weasels, genets, hares, and deer are documented wildlife encountered along the Via Algarviana trail — wild boar specifically present across all zones including mountain areas, representing potential dog-interaction hazard.
stance_target: wild boar as territorial hazard across multiple Algarve zones
scope_conditions: Via Algarviana route, all three Algarve zones
quoted_support: Wild animals will also cross the paths of hikers. Colonies of bees and their keepers. Snakes and scorpions, mostly lying dead on a lonely tarred road. Geckos and chameleons, wild boar, foxes, weasels, genets, hares and perhaps a deer too.

claim: The Via Algarviana trail crosses the A22 motorway underpass (São Bartolomeu de Messines) and railway lines — confirming infrastructure crossing points relevant to barrier/attractor modeling for dog movement.
stance_target: road/rail crossing points as specific infrastructure features for territorial model
scope_conditions: Via Algarviana route near São Bartolomeu de Messines
quoted_support: we also walk under the motorway and cross the railway lines of the express trains near São Bartolomeu de Messines

claim: Large firebreak corridors (hours-long walking stretches) exist near Cachopo (Tavira municipality) — firebreaks are open, cleared linear features that function as natural corridors for dog movement.
stance_target: firebreaks as movement corridors in territorial model
quoted_support: walk for hours on end through firebreaks near Cachopo (Tavira)

claim: Industrial eucalyptus plantations are present 'throughout Portugal' and accompany any walk through the Algarve — eucalyptus plantations are a ubiquitous landscape feature that is traversable but offers poor food/water resources for lost dogs.
stance_target: eucalyptus as widespread traversable but resource-poor terrain layer
quoted_support: Industrial eucalyptus plantations accompany any walk through Portugal.

claim: The Algarve northern zone (Planalto, bordering Alentejo) has springs, mountain streams, lakes and waterfalls — representing permanent water sources that would attract lost dogs in the dry inland areas.
stance_target: northern Algarve Planalto as water-rich zone for dog attraction modeling
quoted_support: The vegetation of the northern Algarve is divided into forest, mountain countryside and scrubland, and fertile arable and grazing land, with springs and mountain streams, lakes and waterfalls.
